segments.title: Science-fiction carriers and parasite craft
segments.summary: The episode defines a science-fiction carrier by whether parasite craft provide its main offensive and defensive reach.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke moves from a real-world fleet-carrier description to a broader test that works for spacecraft: a carrier devotes major volume to operating and recovering parasite craft, and those craft supply its primary attack and defense. The distinction matters because a ship with missiles or guns plus a few fighters is still organized around its shipboard weapons, while a carrier normally stands back and lets its craft create reach. He also notes why this logic can make sense in space, where the large ship remains the best survival platform and the smaller craft extend its defensive and offensive layers.

evidence.note: The transcript shifts from a conventional carrier description to a spacecraft-ready definition based on parasite craft as the main attack and defense system.
Dr. Clarke distinguishes carriers from battleships or battlecruisers with auxiliary fighters, and explains why carrier craft should let the ship stand off.
The later layered-defense discussion clarifies that defensive weapons do not stop a carrier being a carrier if parasite craft remain the most important offensive and defensive layer.
